coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of orange fluorescence multifunctional carbon dots for label-free detection of vitamin B12 and endogenous/exogenous peroxynitrite
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yating
Jiao, Yuan
Zhang, Yuan
Lu, Wenjing
Wang, Xiaodong
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, orange emission fluorescent multifunctional carbon dots (O-CDs) were designed for the label-free detection of vitamin B12 (VB12)，endogenous/exogenous peroxynitrite (ONOO−) sensing, cell imaging, and fluorescent flexible film preparation. The O-CDs with excitation-independent were prepared using safranine T and ethanol as precursors via one-step hydrothermal process. VB12 was utilized as a quencher to quench the fluorescence of O-CDs due to the internal filtration effect (IFE). Two-segment linear ranges are 1–65 μM and 70–140 μM, and the detection limit was calculated as 0.62 μM. Besides, ONOO− can reduce the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs based on static quenching (SQ). The linear ranges are 0.3–9 μM and 9–48 μM, and the detection limit was 0.06 μM. Moreover, the O-CDs were exploited as a cellular imaging reagent for intracellular VB12 and endogenous/exogenous ONOO− imaging owing to its great biocompatibility, low toxicity and strong photostability. These results indicate that O-CDs have the potential to be used as a sensitive fluorescence probe to rapidly monitor VB12 and endogenous/exogenous ONOO− with high selectivity in living cells. Also, the as-proposed O-CDs can be employed to fabricate O-CDs/PVA composites as fluorescent flexible films. All of the above prove that the O-CDs present great prospect in multiple applications such as biosensing, cellular labeling, biomedical optical imaging, and fluorescent films.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Orange emission
Multifunctional carbon dots
Vitamin B12
Peroxynitrite
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent flexible film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram illustration of the fabrication of the O-CDs and CDs-based label-free fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of vitamin B12 and endogenous/exogenous ONOO−. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) TEM, HRTEM (inset) images, (B) the size distribution for O-CDs.
(A) FTIR spectrum; (B) XPS full-survey and high-resolution XPS of (C) C1s and (D) O1s for O-CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra, (B) λex-dependent PL behavior and (C) CIE from PL spectra of O-CDs.

(A) Photoluminescence quenching of O-CDs in the presence of ONOO− (0–81 μM), the inset is relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and ONOO− concentration in the range of 0–81 μM. The linear relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and ONOO− concentrations (B) 0.3–9 μM and (C) 9–48 μM. (D) The influence of ions on the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs. (E) UV–vis spectrum of ONOO−, O-CDs and O-CDs/ONOO−. (F) Fluorescence decay curves of O-CDs and O-CDs/ONOO.

(A) PL spectra of O-CDs upon addition of VB12, the inset is relationship between (F0 – F)/F0 and VB12 concentration in the range of 0–300 μM. A linear correlation of (F0 – F)/F0 values versus VB12 concentration in the range of (B) 1–65 μM and (C) 70–140 μM. (D) The influence of amino acid and vitamins on the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs. (E) UV–vis absorption of VB12 and PL spectra of O-CDs. (F) Fluorescence decay of O-CDs in the absence and presence of VB12.

Confocal fluorescence images of ONOO− in PC-12 cells: (a1–c1) cells incubated with only O-CDs for 30 min; (a2–c2) cells preincubated with SIN-1 for 30 min, then treated with O-CDs for another 30 min; (a3–c3) cells preincubated with LPS for 30 min, then treated with O-CDs for another 30 min. Scale bar is 20 µm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal fluorescence imaging of PC-12 cells incubated with O-CDs and O-CDs/VB12. Scale bar is 20 µm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The photographs of O-CDs/PVA fluorescent flexible film with different content of O-CDs (0%, 1%, 2%, 3% and 4%) under day light and UV light (365 nm).
Comparison of the fluorescence behavior of O-CDs with other carbon dots based ONOO− sensors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Trp-CD = tryptophan doped carbon dots; CDs = carbon dots; PN-CDs = phosphorus and nitrogen doped carbon dots; C-dots = carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the fluorescence behavior of O-CDs with other carbon dots based ONOO− sensors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: t-CD = thermally-reduced carbon dot; CQDs = carbon quantum dots; BCQDs = biomimetic carbon quantum dots; CDs = carbon dots; N,S-CQDs = nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots; s-N-CDs = saccharomyces-derived N-doped carbon dots; PN-CQDs = phosphorus and nitrogen dually-doped carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of orange fluorescence multifunctional carbon dots for label-free detection of vitamin B12 and endogenous/exogenous peroxynitrite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, orange emission fluorescent multifunctional carbon dots (O-CDs) were designed for the label-free detection of vitamin B12 (VB12)，endogenous/exogenous peroxynitrite (ONOO−) sensing, cell imaging, and fluorescent flexible film preparation. The O-CDs with excitation-independent were prepared using safranine T and ethanol as precursors via one-step hydrothermal process. VB12 was utilized as a quencher to quench the fluorescence of O-CDs due to the internal filtration effect (IFE). Two-segment linear ranges are 1–65 μM and 70–140 μM, and the detection limit was calculated as 0.62 μM. Besides, ONOO− can reduce the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs based on static quenching (SQ). The linear ranges are 0.3–9 μM and 9–48 μM, and the detection limit was 0.06 μM. Moreover, the O-CDs were exploited as a cellular imaging reagent for intracellular VB12 and endogenous/exogenous ONOO− imaging owing to its great biocompatibility, low toxicity and strong photostability. These results indicate that O-CDs have the potential to be used as a sensitive fluorescence probe to rapidly monitor VB12 and endogenous/exogenous ONOO− with high selectivity in living cells. Also, the as-proposed O-CDs can be employed to fabricate O-CDs/PVA composites as fluorescent flexible films. All of the above prove that the O-CDs present great prospect in multiple applications such as biosensing, cellular labeling, biomedical optical imaging, and fluorescent films.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their robust photostability (Bao et al., 2015; Park et al., 2020), good biocompatibility (Ding et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2020) and lower cytotoxicity (Jiang et al., 2015; Ye et al., 2019), indicating wide potential applications in photocatalysis (Hu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2014), bioimaging (Bourlinos et al., 2012; Du et al., 2018; Gong et al., 2017; Sheng-Tao Yang et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2017), drug delivery (Pandey et al., 2013; Tang et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2013), and fluorescence sensing (Cui et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Mahmoud et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). However, most of the reported CDs have maximum blue or green light and excitation-dependent emission, which limits their biological applications owing to the short-wavelength light is harmful to the living cells and has to adopt a series of different excitation light sources to achieve diverse emission colors (Gao et al., 2018; Jiao et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018b, 2017; Yang et al., 2016). Consequently, it is of great and profound significance to achieve long-wavelength excitation-independent emission CDs.

In 2015, Zeng et al. (2015) initially synthesized N and S co-doped orange emission CDs using l-cystine and l-serine as raw materials under hydrothermal conditions through a one-pot polycondensation and carbonization reaction and realized the mouse peritoneum macrophage imaging, which provided a new perspective for the preparation of long-wavelength emission CDs. Subsequently, some single-function orange emitting CDs were also developed and exploited in bioimaging and white-light-emitting diodes (WLED) (Qu et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017). In order to further enrich their application, some CDs have been doped as bifunctional sensors. Zhong et al. (2017) prepared orange luminescent carbon dots by one-step hydrothermal method using 1,2-phenylenediamine and amino formaldehyde as raw materials for Ag+ sensing and cell imaging. Up to date, the application of bi-functional orange-emitting CDs has been extended to detect pH (Ding et al., 2018a; Jiao et al., 2018), glucose (Mutuyimana et al., 2018), TNP (Ren et al., 2018), and cell imaging. And multifunctional carbon dots have also been involved for multitask purpose. Jiao et al. (2019) cleverly constructed a ratio fluorescence sensing platform based on orange emission CDs for ratio detection of Ag+ and GSH and cell imaging, which gave a new sight for the multifunctional application of orange emission CDs. Kumari et al. (2019) expanded the applications of the synthesized manganese doped orange emissive CDs in WLED, fingerprinting, fluorescent ink, and bioimaging. In summary, construction of a fluorescent platform with more functions based on orange emission CDs is a research hotspot.

Peroxynitrite (ONOO−) is one of the main reactive oxygen species/reactive nitrogen species (Hou et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2018a). Studies have shown that aberrant levels of ONOO− can raise the risk of various diseases (Zhu et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2020). Therefore, it is meaningful and important to exploit highly selective and sensitive probes for ONOO− sensing. At present, many techniques have been exploited for ONOO− sensing, such as electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy (de Menezes and Augusto, 2001), UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy (Li et al., 2004), electrochemical analysis (Hosu et al., 2016), and chromatography (Beatriz Alvarez et al., 1996). Fluorescence method has been also applied for ONOO– sensing due to its highly sensitive. Some fluorescent probes from carbon dots have been developed for sensing of ONOO−. An analytical method based on tryptophan-doped blue emission carbon dots was first developed to detect ONOO− in real samples, which opened a new horizon for the fluorescent sensing of carbon dots (Simões et al., 2014). Subsequently, some researches also employed CDs as fluorescent probes capable of detecting ONOO− in real samples (Simões et al., 2015; Eliana et al., 2016). The achievement of intracellular detection of ONOO− is more beneficial, but this is still a great challenge. Gong et al.Gong et al., (2016) fabricated blue luminescence P, N-CDs as a novel sensing platform for cell imaging of endogenous ONOO− in macrophages. However, all of these ONOO− sensors are based on short-wavelength carbon dots, which limits the application of carbon dots in living organisms. Thus, it sorely needs to develop an endogenous/exogenous ONOO− sensing strategy on the basis of long-wavelength CDs carbon dots in biosystem.

Vitamin B12 (VB12) is one of the B vitamins and plays an important role in human health. Lack of VB12 can lead to anemia, abnormal metabolism and mental illness. Elevated VB12 can raise the risk of liver disease, abnormal bone marrow hyperplasia or kidney failure. As such, the monitoring of VB12 in living cells is crucial in chemistry and biomedical fields. At present, several VB12 sensors based on CDs have been exploited. Qiu’s group (Wang et al., 2015) first developed a novel thermally-reduced blue emission carbon dots based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensor for the determination of VB12 in aqueous solutions, which provides a novel idea for fluorescence sensing applications of CDs. After this work, several methods have been developed for detection of VB12 based on CDs in real samples (Kalaiyarasan and Joseph, 2017; Ding et al., 2018b). However, VB12 analysis in biological systems is still challenging. Wang et al. (2018) synthesized highly fluorescent biomimetic blue emission carbon quantum dots for label-free sensitive and selective detection of VB12 in Hela cells, which expand its hidden use of CDs in living cells. However, all of this method for VB12 sensing are based on short-wavelength emission carbon dots, which severely limits the application in the field of biological imaging. Thus, it is indispensable to manufacture a VB12 sensor from long-wavelength emission CDs in biological systems.

Herein, orange emission fluorescent multifunctional carbon dots (O-CDs) were prepared using safranine T and ethanol as precursors via one-step hydrothermal process for label-free detection of vitamin B12 and endogenous/exogenous ONOO−. VB12 was capable to decrease the fluorescence of O-CDs owing to internal filtration effect (IFE). Meanwhile, ONOO− was utilized as a quencher to quench the fluorescence of O-CDs based on static quenching (SQ). The O-CDs also possess a variety of excellent properties including tremendous water dispersibility, good biocompatibility, an excitation-independent emission and lower cytotoxicity, which empowering O-CDs with a potential application for noninvasively assessment of intracellular VB12 and endogenous/exogenous ONOO− in biosystem. Moreover, the as-proposed O-CDs can be employed to fabricate O-CDs/PVA composites as fluorescent flexible films. It is prospective that the as-fabricated O-CDs as a promising nanomaterial can be applied in multiple applications such as biosensing, cellular labeling, biomedical optical imaging, and photovoltaic materials.

Safranine T, ethanol, amino acids, vitamins, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, molecular weight: 1500), and lipopolysaccharides (LPS) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). 3-morpholinosydnonimine (SIN-1) was obtained from Alfa Chemistry (New York, USA). Other reagents were taken from Sigma (Shanghai, China). All purchased reagents were of analytical grade and used as received from the commercial suppliers without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.25 MO cm) for all experiments was acquired by a Milli-Q Plus system.

First, 0.25 g of safranine T were accurately quantified in the beaker and 20 mL ethanol were added, keep stirring until the safranine T completely dissolved. The solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and then heated to 200 °C for 6 h. Then, the autoclave was cooled down at room temperature naturally. Subsequently, the product was diluted 5 times by ultrapure water. The solution of produced R-CDs was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove any deposits. Next, the supernatant was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1/4 500−1000) for 72 h after filtering by a 0.22 µm microporous membrane. Last, the solid-state R-CDs were collected by freeze-drying.

Initially, various analytes were added into 1.8 mL PBS solution and 200 μL stock solution of O-CDs (0.62 mg/mL) to check the selectivity. For ONOO− analysis, different concentrations of ONOO− was mixed with stock solution of O-CDs. The PL detection was performed in PBS solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4) at room temperature. The PL spectra were recorded at excitation wavelength of 545 nm.

In a typical assay, 200 μL of O-CDs (0.62 mg/mL) were diluted to 1.8 mL PBS solution of pH 7.4. For VB12 assay, different concentration of VB12 was mixed with O-CDs solutions. The FL spectra were recorded at excitation wavelength of 545 nm. All the experiments were implemented in triplicate.

Three groups of rat adrenal medulla pheochromocytoma differentiated cells PC-12 cells were hatched in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplied with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). The PC-12 cells (0.25 mg/mL) incubated with O-CDs were used as a control group, and then PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4) was used to wash the cells three times. For imaging of exogenous ONOO−, 3-morpholinosydnonimine (SIN-1, 1 mM) was used to pretreat the cells for 30 min to provide exogenous ONOO−, meanwhile the endogenous ONOO− was stimulated by adding lipopolysaccharides (LPS, 1 mM). After incubation at 37 °C for 60 min, the cells were washed three times in PBS (pH=7.4). The fluorescence image was collected on a confocal laser scanning microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent flexible film
ce:section.ce:para: 5 g of PVA added into 50 mL of ultrapure water and stir vigorously at 90 ℃ until a clear solution is obtained. After cooling to room temperature, the PVA solution was mixed with O-CDs with different contents. The O-CDs solutions were dropped into the molds and dried at 60 °C for 1 day. The fluorescent flexible films were obtained.

The orange emission fluorescent carbon dots (O-CDs) were prepared using safranine T and ethanol as precursors via one-step hydrothermal process at 200 ℃ for 6 h ( Fig. 1). In the early stage of the reaction, the hydrothermal temperature, reaction time and contents of safranine T related to the fluorescence of O-CDs have been discussed in detail. As illustrated in Fig. S1A and S1B, the fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of O-CDs reach to the optimal values under the reaction temperature of 200 ℃ for 6 h. In addition, the effect of safranine T content on the fluorescence of O-CDs was demonstrated in Fig. S1C, it can be found that the QY of O-CDs increases gradually with the introduction of safranine T and attain the maximum with the amount of 0.025 g.

The TEM technique are examined to investigate structure and morphology ( Fig. 2A). The as-prepared O-CDs are nearly uniform and monodisperse with the average diameter of 2.04 nm (Fig. 2B). The High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 2A) reveals the interlayer lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, which represents the (100) diffraction planes of graphitic structure (Ding et al., 2015).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are performed to further analyze the surface functional groups and chemical structure of O-CDs. The FTIR analysis ( Fig. 3A) demonstrates that the characteristic stretching vibration of O–H, N–H, C˭O and C–H are observed at 3,326 cm−1, 3,160 cm−1, 1,639 cm−1 and 1,483 cm−1, respectively. The absorption peak at 1,182 and 1,015 cm−1 correspond to C–O. The full range XPS spectra (Fig. 3B) exhibits three peaks at 280.1, 396.4 and 527.0 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) exhibits three obvious characteristic peaks of sp2 carbons (C–C/C˭C), sp3 carbons (C−O/C−N) and carboxyl carbons (COOH) appearing at 284.9, 286.0 and 288.2 eV. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) shows three binding energies at 399.3 and 400.8 eV, which can be assigned to amino-N and pyrrolic-N, respectively. The O1s band in Fig. S2 can be resolved into two doublets of C˭O (532.0 eV) and C–O (533.4 eV). The results fit well with the FTIR data and illustrate that the O-CDs surface is equipped with –OH and –NH2 as functional groups, which makes O-CDs have exceptional dispersibility in aqueous solutions.

The optical properties of O-CDs are verified by UV–vis absorption and PL spectra. The O-CDs display double peaks at 248 and 273 nm ( Fig. 4A), which attribute to π→π* transition of the C˭C bond and the n→π* transition of the C−N or C−O/C˭O bond. The wide absorption band at 518 nm could be denoted as the functional groups of the precursor safranine T remaining on the surface of O-CDs. The 3D emission profiles of the as-prepared O-CDs (Fig. 4B) shows that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are 545 and 595 nm, respectively. And the emission wavelength of O-CDs is independent of the excitation wavelength. Chromaticity diagram (CIE) was explored to demonstrate the PL property of the O-CDs (Fig. 4C). The CIE coordinates are calculated as (0.6175, 0.3821), which proves the potential use of O-CDs for biosensing. The quantum yield of O-CDs at 545 nm is calculated to be 7.6% in aqueous solution under optimal excitation, using cresyl violet as the reference (Fig. S3). Moreover, the O-CDs manifest high anti-photobleaching property for photoluminescence intensity remains invariant under continuously Xenon lamp irradiated with 60 min (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, the high ionic strength (4.0 M) and wide pH range (2−12) have no noticeable change for photoluminescence intensity of O-CDs, which certificates more latent use of O-CDs in a complex environment and living systems.

Since O-CDs has functional groups on the surface, we explored its potential applications in fluorescent probes. In Fig. 5A, with increase in the ONOO− concentration, a gradual decrease of PL intensity of O-CDs could be observed. The linear correlation was obtained over ONOO− concentration of 0.3–9 μM (Fig. 5B, y = 0.0250x + 0.0063, R2 = 0.9901) and 9–48 μM (Fig. 5C, y = 0.0082x + 0.1640, R2 = 0.9921). And the LOD was 0.06 μM. To further assess the selectivity of O-CDs towards ONOO−, it was investigated that the fluorescence responses of O-CDs to various ions（NO3 −, S2−, Br−, Cl−, NO2 −, C2O4 2−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, F−, SO3 2−, SCN−, S2O3 2−). As shown in Fig. 5D, only ONOO− result in the fluorescence emission of O-CDs slightly decrease, while the other interferers show no obvious change. These results confirmed that O-CDs possess excellent selectivity toward ONOO−. Compared with other fluorescent ONOO− sensor based on carbon dots, the O-CDs possess longer fluorescence emission, which make it more valuable for ONOO− measurements in biological systems in the future ( Table 1).

Certain reactions might occur between ONOO− and CDs which was rich in amino groups (Zhu et al., 2017). A possible sensing mechanism to peroxynitrite with fluorescence quenching of O-CDs was explored by using UV–vis spectrum and fluorescence lifetime. As described in Fig. 5E, the absorption peak of ONOO− at 302 nm, which leads to the increase of the absorption peak of O-CDs at 273 nm, the decrease of the absorption peak at 518 nm, and the appearance of a new peak at 374 nm after the introduction of ONOO−. It may be that the functional group on the surface of O-CDs is combined with ONOO−. More importantly, before and after addition of ONOO−, the lifetime of O-CDs has not changed significantly (from 3.46 ns to 3.35 ns). It is speculated that the photoluminescence quenching may owing to static quenching (SQ). According to Fig. S5, the quenching rate constant Kq can be calculated to be 7.43 * 1012 L mol−1 s−1, which much greater than 2 * 1010 L mol−1 s−1. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching is strongly attributed to static quenching.

Due to the unique nature on the surface of O-CDs, we were also prompted to investigate the potential sensing applications. Fig. 6A shows that as the concentration of VB12 increases, the fluorescence is clearly quenched to 15% of the original photoluminescence intensity. There was a great linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (F0-F/F0) and VB12 concentration in the range of 1–65 μM (Fig. 6B, y = 0.0061x + 0.0087, R2 = 0.9961) and 70–140 μM (Fig. 6C, y = 0.0033x + 0.1834, R2 = 0.9971). The LOD is 0.62 μM. As selectivity is an major characteristic, the selectivity experiments of O-CDs for VB12 were then conducted. Fig. 6D shows no obvious fluorescence changes after the addition of some amino acids and vitamins. Only VB12 induced significant fluorescence quenching, which proved the high selectivity of the prepared O-CDs for VB12 analysis. Compared with other fluorescent method of VB12 assay based on carbon dots, O-CDs has a longer fluorescence emission time, which makes it more potentially valuable for VB12 analysis in biological systems ( Table 2).

UV–vis absorption spectra and lifetime measurement were performed to demonstrate the response mechanism. As shown in Fig. 6E, the absorption spectrum of VB12 absorbed the excitation spectrum of O-CDs, demonstrating the IFE may result in the fluorescence quenched. Additionally, fluorescence lifetime data was obtained to further comprehend the fluorescence response mechanism. As described in Fig. 6F, The fluorescence lifetimes are 5.23 ns (O-CDs) and 5.22 ns (O-CDs/VB12), respectively. The average life expectancy is almost unchanged, confirming that the PL quenching is IFE.

The cytotoxicity of O-CDs was assessed through standard MTT assay using PC-12 cells as the model. The survival rate of PC-12 cells (Fig. S6) was more than 80% after incubating with O-CDs (0–300 μg/mL), which demonstrated O-CDs had low toxicity and inferred its potential application for bioimaging.

The next experiments were done to determine whether O-CDs could be successfully used for the visible detection of exogenous/endogenous ONOO− in living cells. As displayed in Fig. 7, the PC-12 cells showed strong orange fluorescence after incubation with O-CDs. To monitor exogenous peroxynitrite in living cells, 3-morpholinosydnonimine (SIN-1) was added to culture medium in advance 30 min to generate ONOO−. The orange fluorescence of cells has been quenched after incubating with O-CDs for 30 min, indicating that O-CDs can be utilized to image the exogenous peroxynitrite anion in living cells. Simultaneously, the PC-12 cells were stimulated with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) for 4 h to produce endogenous ONOO−, then incubated with O-CDs for 30 min. As expected, the cells stimulated with LPS exhibited a faint orange fluorescence. This proves the potential application of O-CDs for monitoring of exogenous/endogenous ONOO− in living cells.

We also explore the latent applications of O-CDs for monitoring VB12 in living cells. As displayed in Fig. 8, after incubating PC-12 cells with the O-CDs (0.25 mg/mL) for 30 min, significant orange emission in the cytoplasm region can be clearly observed, which proves that O-CDs is internalized and fully dispersed into the cytoplasmic area. When VB12 enters the PC-12 cell nutrient solution, the orange fluorescence gradually weakens. The previous information suggested that the O-CDs could be utilized to monitor VB12 in living cells.

To confirm the applicability of O-CDs as fluorescent flexible film, PVA was selected to fabricate O-CDs/polymer composites. Fig. 9 is the photographs of O-CDs/PVA film with different O-CDs (0–4%) under day light and UV light. When the content of O-CDs increased, the fluorescence increased gradually accompanied. In brief, the as-fabricated O-CDs were greatly advantageous to fabricate photovoltaic materials.

In summary, a low-cost and environmentally friendly strategy has been used to synthesize orange emission multifunctional carbon dots (O-CDs) with independent PL behavior. O-CDs have outstanding characteristics of high sensitivity, rapid response time, wide response range, and excellent biocompatibility, which making them utilized for in vivo vitamin B12 and ONOO– detection and cell imaging. As proposed O-CDs can be exploited for label-free, sensitive and real-time monitoring of vitamin B12 and endogenous/exogenous ONOO− from internal filtration effect (IFE) and static quenching (SQ), respectively. Interestingly, O-CDs are also used to manufacture O-CDs/PVA composites as fluorescent flexible films. All of the above prove that the O-CDs presents great prospect in multiple applications such as biosensing, cellular labeling, and photovoltaic materials.

Yating Meng: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yuan Jiao: Investigation, Discussed the results. Yuan Zhang: Formal analysis, Discussed the results. Wenjing Lu: Project administration, Supervision. Xiaodong Wang: Supervision. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087), The Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province under Grant (No. 201901D211155) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2017 Key 1).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.124422.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence-tunable carbon nanodots: surface-state energy-gap tuning
Adv. Mater.

Chem. Res. Toxicol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gd(iii)-doped carbon dots as a dual fluorescent-MRI probe
